skill.name: golang-error-handling
skill.description: Guides idiomatic Go error handling, including error creation, wrapping, inspection, joining, panic recovery, structured logging, and production error context.
skill.summary: Improve Go error creation, wrapping, inspection, and structured logging.
skill.author: samber

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and sensitive-file patterns. Manual review found these are markdown formatting, Go documentation examples, reference URLs, and scoped Go tooling instructions, not malicious behavior. No prompt injection or credential exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Scoped Bash and Agent Usage
low_findings.description: The skill allows Bash for go, golangci-lint, and git commands, and it suggests sub-agents for audits. This is legitimate for a Go coding skill, but users should still apply normal workspace permissions and review generated commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration directly permits scoped command execution. The commands are limited to normal Go development tools and no malicious intent is present.

low_findings.title: External Reference Links
low_findings.description: The skill includes GitHub and Go package documentation links. These are normal references and do not instruct the agent to fetch or transmit user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references only. No runtime network call, webhook, credential transfer, or data collection instruction was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static External Command Matches Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: Most external command alerts are markdown inline code, fenced Go examples, and eval prompts. They describe Go error handling APIs and anti-patterns rather than asking the agent to execute shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose, JSON prompt text, or fenced Go snippets. No shell metacharacter execution path or user-controlled command construction was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to crypto APIs or algorithms. The cited content discusses Go error wrapping, error codes, and gateway error-chain exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use at these locations. The matches appear to be lexical false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reconnaissance and Sensitive File Matches Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The system reconnaissance and SQLite-file alerts are examples about reading config files, stack traces, and database insert errors. They do not instruct host enumeration, secret discovery, or database file access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are ordinary Go error-handling snippets. No credential harvesting, filesystem reconnaissance workflow, or SQLite database file path was present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fetch API Matches Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The network fetch alerts in the wrapping reference are from a Go method named fetch in a database example. This is not a JavaScript Fetch API call and does not perform network exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited code is a Go pseudo-example using s.repo.fetch and fmt.Errorf. There is no fetch API, URL target, or data transmission.

content.value_statement: Go error handling becomes hard to maintain when errors are swallowed, duplicated in logs, or wrapped without useful context. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical rules for creating, wrapping, inspecting, joining, and logging errors in production Go code.
actual_capabilities: Guides use of errors.New, fmt.Errorf, sentinel errors, and custom error types.
Explains when to wrap with %w and when to hide internals with %v.
Reviews errors.Is, errors.As, errors.AsType, and errors.Join patterns.
Applies the single handling rule to avoid duplicate log-and-return behavior.
Recommends slog and structured error context for production observability.
Provides audit categories for checking error handling across Go codebases.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not guarantee a complete automated refactor.
Some recommendations depend on Go version support, especially errors.AsType.
samber/oops guidance assumes the project accepts that dependency.
It does not replace security review for sensitive error messages or logs.

use_cases.title: Review a Go Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check changed code for swallowed errors, missing wrapping context, direct error comparisons, and duplicate logging.

use_cases.title: Refactor Service Errors
use_cases.description: Update service methods to wrap errors with context, use structured attributes, and keep user-facing messages safe.

use_cases.title: Audit Production Logging
use_cases.description: Find log-and-return patterns, high-cardinality messages, and missing structured fields that reduce observability quality.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small Go function
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Go function for idiomatic error handling. Check whether every returned error is handled, wrapped with useful context, and written with lowercase error text.

prompt_templates.title: Modernize Error Wrapping
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a package that returns bare errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Go package so errors are created and wrapped idiomatically. Use errors.Is or errors.As where matching is needed, and explain each behavioral change.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Logging and Observability
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Go service for log-and-return violations, high-cardinality error messages, and unstructured logging. Propose changes using slog and structured attributes.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Full Error Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Go codebase for error creation, wrapping, chain inspection, panic recovery, structured logging, and user-safe error boundaries. Return prioritized findings and fixes.

output_examples.input: A repository method returns raw database errors and compares errors with direct equality.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend wrapping database failures with method context and using errors.Is for sentinel matching. It would also flag direct comparison because wrapping can hide the original sentinel.

output_examples.input: A worker logs an error, then returns the same error to the caller.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify a single handling rule violation. It would advise returning the wrapped error and logging once at the top-level boundary.

output_examples.input: A batch import needs to report many validation failures.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend collecting independent validation errors with errors.Join and attaching row or field details as structured context where appropriate.

best_practices: Wrap errors with stable context before returning them from lower layers.
Handle each error once by logging it or returning it, not both.
Keep log messages stable and place variable data in structured attributes.
anti_patterns: Discarding errors with blank identifiers or using successful values before checking errors.
Interpolating user IDs, paths, or row numbers into stable production error messages.
Using panic for expected failures such as validation errors, timeouts, or missing rows.

faq.question: Does this skill write Go code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide edits for Go error handling patterns when used inside Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require samber/oops?
faq.answer: No. It explains standard library patterns first and recommends samber/oops when structured production errors are useful.

faq.question: When should %w be used?
faq.answer: Use %w inside a module when callers should inspect the error chain. Use %v at boundaries when internals should stay hidden.

faq.question: Can it help with logging?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends slog, stable messages, structured attributes, and one top-level handling point for each error.

faq.question: Can it audit a whole codebase?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes audit categories for creation, wrapping, panic recovery, logging, and single handling rule checks.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Manual review found no malicious instructions. It has low risk because it permits scoped Go development commands and includes reference links.
